title: Statistical challenges for inferring multiple SARS-CoV-2 spillovers with early outbreak phylodynamics (preprint critique)

claims.claim: The paper argues that the phylodynamic model used by Pekar et al. is biased against polytomies, so Pekar et al.'s finding of a low probability of basal (Lineage A and B) polytomies under a single-introduction scenario is a model artifact rather than genuine evidence for two spillovers.
claims.quote: their phylodynamic model is biased against polytomies, making their finding of a low probability of basal polytomies an artifact of their model

claims.claim: The paper contends that Pekar et al. improperly excluded 20 SARS-CoV-2 genomes with intermediate (A-B) haplotypes using questionable criteria (low read depth, claims of erroneous base calls at lineage-defining sites), and that these intermediate genomes are inconsistent with a strict two-spillover model that requires lineages A and B to arise from separate introductions.
claims.quote: Pekar et al. identified and excluded 20 A-B intermediate genomes from their analysis, applying a variety of exclusion criteria including low read depth and assertions of repeated erroneous base calls at lineage-defining positions

claims.claim: The critique identifies two specific methodological artifacts that it says drive Pekar et al.'s two-spillover conclusion: the exclusion of informative genomes and reliance on unrealistic phylodynamic models; correcting these undermines the quantitative support for multiple introductions.
claims.quote: two methodological artifacts impacting Pekar et al.'s findings: the dubious exclusion of informative SARS-CoV-2 genomes, and their reliance on unrealistic phylodynamic models of SARS-CoV-2

claims.claim: The paper concludes that the phylogenetic/phylodynamic evidence does not robustly distinguish two independent zoonotic introductions from a single introduction, because the analyses fail to account for ascertainment and sampling biases in how early Wuhan cases were detected and sampled.
claims.quote: the earlier papers are inconclusive as they fail to account for biases in how medical managers became alerted to SARS-CoV-2 and how public health authorities sampled early cases, and that key data points conflicting with the authors' conclusions were improperly excluded

title: Animal Source Most Likely Origin But Missing Chinese Data Leave Findings Inconclusive: WHO SAGO (Health Policy Watch)

claims.claim: The WHO Scientific Advisory Group for the Origins of Novel Pathogens (SAGO) concluded that the weight of scientific evidence supports SARS-CoV-2 first entering humans either directly from bats or via an intermediate host, making a zoonotic (animal-source) origin the most likely explanation.
claims.quote: most scientific data and accessible published scientific evidence [support the hypothesis that SARS-CoV-2 first entered the human population either directly from virus-carrying bats, or from bats to humans via intermediate hosts]

claims.claim: SAGO received information (from China CDC) of molecular evidence that animals sold at the Huanan market, including raccoon dogs, were susceptible to SARS-CoV-2, and DNA from such susceptible wildlife was found in SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples from the market.
claims.quote: molecular evidence that some of the animals sold at the Huanan Market, including raccoon dogs, "were susceptible to SARS CoV2"

claims.claim: Despite favoring an animal origin, SAGO stated the origins of SARS-CoV-2 will remain inconclusive until more data becomes available, and it could not rule out a laboratory-related origin, largely because of data gaps attributed to China.
claims.quote: the origins of SARS CoV2 will remain inconclusive until more scientific data becomes available

claims.claim: A key barrier to a definitive conclusion is unshared Chinese data — including genetic sequences from Wuhan patient samples and 'upstream' information on the farms and traders supplying animals to Wuhan markets — which also constrains investigation of the lab-leak hypothesis.
claims.quote: large gaps in data provided by China have confounded investigation of the lab leak hypothesis

title: Bloom (2023), Association between SARS-CoV-2 and metagenomic content of samples from the Huanan Seafood Market, Virus Evolution

claims.claim: In the Huanan market environmental samples, SARS-CoV-2 reads are NOT consistently correlated with reads from susceptible non-human animals like raccoon dogs; samples with abundant raccoon-dog material usually contain little or no SARS-CoV-2, undercutting the inference that these samples pinpoint an infected animal.
claims.quote: the samples that contain abundant material from raccoon dogs and other non-human susceptible species usually contain little or no SARS-CoV-2 reads. More specifically, the number of SARS-CoV-2 reads is not consistently correlated with reads mapping to non-human susceptible species but is instead generally most correlated with reads from species that are implausible candidates for having been infected with SARS-CoV-2.

claims.claim: The co-mingling of viral and animal genetic material in the January 2020 market environmental samples is unlikely to reveal which species (if any) was an intermediate host, because the virus was already widespread in the market by then.
claims.quote: These results suggest that SARS-CoV-2 was widespread in the market by January 2020 and therefore that co-mingling of viral and animal genetic material in environmental samples collected at that time is unlikely to be informative about zoonotic origins.

claims.claim: Bloom's reanalysis and the original (China CDC / Crits-Christoph et al.) analysis agree on the underlying data: the market samples contain genetic material from many species, including raccoon dogs, which are susceptible to SARS-CoV-2. The disagreement is over interpretation, not the presence of susceptible-species DNA.
claims.quote: Both studies concurred that the samples contained genetic material from a variety of species, including some like raccoon dogs that are susceptible to SARS-CoV-2.

claims.claim: The environmental samples analyzed were collected only after the market was closed on 1 January 2020, meaning they postdate the earliest human cases and cannot directly establish an animal source of the initial spillover.
claims.quote: the Chinese Centers for Disease Control (CDC) released data from deep sequencing of environmental samples collected from the market after it was closed on 1 January 2020

title: The SARS-CoV-2 furin cleavage site was not engineered (2022), PNAS

claims.claim: The 8-amino-acid similarity (RRARSVAS) between the SARS-CoV-2 spike furin cleavage site and a human ENaC furin cleavage site is coincidental, not the product of engineering.
claims.quote: The short amino acid similarity is quite simply happenstance.

claims.claim: Codon usage rules out ENaC-mimicry engineering: except for a single codon (cgu encoding arginine 685), every codon encoding RRARSVAS differs between human ENaC and SARS-CoV-2, which would not be expected if the sequence had been copied from ENaC.
claims.quote: except for one codon (cgu that encodes arginine 685), each of the codons for RRARSVAS is different in human ENaC and SARS-CoV-2

claims.claim: Five of the eight amino acids (RSVAS) in/near the shared ENaC-like motif are already present in the spikes of naturally occurring sarbecoviruses such as BANAL-20-52, so the motif arises naturally in related coronaviruses rather than requiring a human-ENaC template.
claims.quote: five of eight amino acids (RSVAS) in or near the ENaC FCS sequence shared with SARS-CoV-2 Spike are present in Spikes of sarbecoviruses, such as BANAL-20-52

claims.claim: The paper directly rebuts Harrison and Sachs' accusation that UNC and Wuhan Institute of Virology coronavirologists mimicked the human ENaC furin cleavage site to make SARS-CoV-2 more infectious for lung epithelia; a designer would illogically have used a coronavirus FCS rather than ENaC's.
claims.quote: it would be illogical to use the FCS from ENac rather than from a FCS of another coronavirus

title: Report on Potential Links Between the Wuhan Institute of Virology and the Origins of COVID-19 (ODNI, June 23, 2023)

claims.claim: The US Intelligence Community assessed that SARS-CoV-2 was almost certainly not genetically engineered: most agencies concluded with low confidence that it probably was not engineered, while two agencies found insufficient evidence to assess either way. This weighs against the specific 'engineered bioweapon' variant of the lab-leak hypothesis.
claims.quote: Most agencies assess with low confidence that SARS-CoV-2 probably was not genetically engineered; however, two agencies believe there was not sufficient evidence to make an assessment either way.

claims.claim: The report confirms that the Wuhan Institute of Virology had the technical capability to create and genetically engineer novel coronaviruses (including chimeras), establishing that a lab-origin pathway was technically feasible even though the IC found no evidence it occurred.
claims.quote: The brief confirmed that the Wuhan Institute of Virology is capable of seamlessly engineering new coronaviruses.

claims.claim: Several WIV staff were ill with respiratory or other symptoms in the fall of 2019, but the IC judged these illnesses did not clearly point to COVID-19, so the 'sick researchers' reporting neither confirms nor refutes a lab-associated origin.
claims.quote: "Several" staff at the lab were sick in November, with "some of their symptoms were consistent but not diagnostic of COVID-19".

claims.claim: The declassified IC assessment did not identify direct evidence that the WIV possessed SARS-CoV-2 or a close progenitor virus before the pandemic, leaving the central lab-leak mechanism unproven by the intelligence available.
claims.quote: Report shows U.S. intelligence on the Wuhan Institute of Virology is thin

claims.claim: The report was mandated by the COVID-19 Origin Act of 2023 and declassified by DNI Avril Haines on 23 June 2023, documenting that the IC remained divided (five agencies plus the National Intelligence Council favoring natural spillover, the FBI and Department of Energy favoring a lab-associated origin).
claims.quote: DECLASSIFIED by DNI Haines on 23 June 2023

title: Pekar et al. (2022), The molecular epidemiology of multiple zoonotic origins of SARS-CoV-2, Science

claims.claim: Prior to February 2020, SARS-CoV-2 genomic diversity comprised only two distinct viral lineages, designated A and B, which cannot be readily explained by a single introduction into humans.
claims.quote: SARS-CoV-2 genomic diversity before February 2020 likely comprised only two distinct viral lineages, denoted 'A' and 'B'

claims.claim: Phylodynamic rooting and epidemic simulations indicate the two lineages resulted from at least two separate cross-species (zoonotic) transmission events into humans, rather than one spillover.
claims.quote: phylodynamic rooting methods, coupled with epidemic simulations, reveal that these lineages were the result of at least two separate cross-species transmission events into humans

claims.claim: The most probable explanation for SARS-CoV-2's emergence in humans is zoonotic jumps from as-yet undetermined intermediate host animals at the Huanan Seafood Market, favoring a market-based zoonotic origin over a lab origin.
claims.quote: The most probable explanation for the introduction of SARS-CoV-2 into humans involves zoonotic jumps from as-yet undetermined, intermediate host animals at the Huanan market

claims.claim: The first zoonotic transmission likely involved lineage B viruses around 18 November 2019 (range 23 October to 8 December 2019), with the separate lineage A introduction occurring within weeks of that event.
claims.quote: The first zoonotic transmission likely involved lineage B viruses around 18 November 2019 (23 October to 8 December), while the separate introduction of lineage A likely occurred within weeks of this event

claims.claim: Statistical analysis of the earliest human cases located them to one section of the Huanan market where live wild-animal vendors congregated and where virus-positive environmental samples were concentrated.
claims.quote: statistically located the earliest human cases to one section where vendors of live wild animals congregated and where virus-positive environmental samples concentrated

title: U.S. Right to Know — NIH files reveal broader coronavirus engineering research before COVID-19

claims.claim: On March 31, 2016, NIH notified the University of Minnesota that a Li-Baric coronavirus grant might fall under the federal gain-of-function funding pause, asking the researchers to clarify whether the work could produce enhanced pathogen traits in mammals via the respiratory route.
claims.quote: On March 31, 2016, NIH notified the University of Minnesota that the Li-Baric grant might include research subject to the funding pause and asked the scientists to clarify whether any proposed work could result in "enhanced pathogenicity and/or transmissibility in mammals via the respiratory route."

claims.claim: A grant component labeled 'Experiment 4' proposed creating live recombinant SARS-like viruses with engineered mutations to test how efficiently the viruses could infect different species.
claims.quote: One component — "Experiment 4" — proposed creating live recombinant SARS-like viruses with mutations designed to test how efficiently the viruses could infect different species.

claims.claim: The 2018 DEFUSE proposal, coauthored by the Wuhan Institute of Virology (Zhengli Shi), EcoHealth Alliance, and UNC's Ralph Baric, proposed engineering high-risk SARS-related coronaviruses including cleavage-site insertions and spike-protein swaps, but was never funded.
claims.quote: Project DEFUSE, a 2018 grant proposal coauthored by the Wuhan Institute of Virology and American scientists, which proposed engineering high-risk coronaviruses of the same species as SARS and SARS-CoV-2

claims.claim: Ralph Baric, a UNC virologist who pioneered coronavirus reverse-genetics (no-see-um / seamless cloning) systems, had a years-long collaboration with Chinese researcher Li and others studying how coronaviruses adapt to new hosts.
claims.quote: Baric, a University of North Carolina virologist widely known for pioneering coronavirus reverse-genetics systems, had collaborated for years with Li and other international researchers studying how coronaviruses adapt to new hosts.

title: A Critical Analysis of the Evidence for the SARS-CoV-2 Origin Hypotheses (2023), Journal of Virology

claims.claim: The two leading origin hypotheses cannot currently be distinguished or falsified with available evidence, so a definitive determination of SARS-CoV-2's origin is unlikely.
claims.quote: The primary candidate hypotheses are not readily falsifiable with currently available information; thus, a definitive determination of the origin is unlikely.

claims.claim: The core reason the origin debate remains unresolved is missing data, not conclusive evidence for either side; the lab-leak hypothesis in particular cannot be confirmed without new evidence.
claims.quote: The origin of SARS-CoV-2 has been the topic of substantial debate primarily because we do not have access to some relevant data. ... Unless evidence is forthcoming, the laboratory leak hypothesis cannot be confirmed.

claims.claim: The intact furin cleavage site in early SARS-CoV-2 isolates argues against a laboratory cell-culture origin, because culturing the virus in cell lines reproducibly deletes the furin cleavage site region of the spike gene.
claims.quote: Hundreds of research groups have cultured human isolates of SARS-CoV-2 in cell lines, revealing a highly reproducible consequence: the deletion of the portion of the spike gene encoding the furin cleavage site. However, early isolates of SARS-CoV-2 show the furin cleavage site to be intact, arguing against introduction into humans after laboratory cell culture.

claims.claim: No single viral genome feature (including the furin cleavage site or RBD ACE2-binding residues) provides definitive evidence for either hypothesis; each is consistent with both natural evolution and laboratory scenarios such as serial passage.
claims.quote: None of the viral genome features alone provides definitive evidence for either hypothesis, and each must be interpreted within the broader context of coronavirus evolution and research methodologies.

claims.claim: An intermediate host is presumed for zoonotic transmission (bats as natural reservoir, pangolins proposed as intermediate), but the identity and even existence of an intermediate host remain controversial and unconfirmed.
claims.quote: Bats have been suggested as the original host species, with pangolins acting as an intermediate animal vector. However, it is controversial whether pangolins are the intermediate host or natural host, or whether they are even a host.

title: COVID-19 lab leak theory — Wikipedia
COVID-origins study links raccoon dogs to Wuhan market: what scientists think (UNMC)
The proximal origin of SARS-CoV-2 (Wikipedia)

claims.claim: The paper "The proximal origin of SARS-CoV-2" (Andersen, Rambaut, Lipkin, Holmes, Garry), published in Nature Medicine on 17 March 2020, concluded that the virus arose by natural zoonotic spillover and explicitly rejected a laboratory origin.
claims.quote: The authors explained that the genomes of SARS-COV-2 and related coronaviruses, and the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, suggested that the virus spilled into human populations naturally via zoonosis, and concluded: "we do not believe that any type of laboratory-based scenario is plausible."

claims.claim: During drafting, the authors privately considered a lab origin (including the furin cleavage site arising via serial passage) and acknowledged the evidence did not rule out an accidental lab infection, in tension with the confident public conclusion.
claims.quote: In internal discussions, Andersen stated "None of this disproves accidental lab infection" and noted it "shows that all the steps can occur in nature."

claims.claim: An early draft of the paper stated that furin cleavage sites can arise in betacoronaviruses in the lab through serial passage, but this discussion was altered before the final published version dismissed the lab scenario.
claims.quote: An early draft stated that furin cleavage sites can arise in betacoronaviruses in the lab through serial passage, but this discussion was changed in the final version.

claims.claim: The paper's case for natural origin rested on specific genomic features — the receptor-binding domain and the spike protein / furin cleavage site — which the authors argued were more consistent with natural selection than with laboratory engineering.
claims.quote: the genomes of SARS-COV-2 and related coronaviruses, and the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, suggested that the virus spilled into human populations naturally via zoonosis, and not through a laboratory leak

title: Newly declassified report shows U.S. intelligence community remains divided over likely origin of Covid (NBC News)

claims.claim: As of the June 2023 declassified report, all U.S. intelligence agencies continued to assess that both a natural and a laboratory-associated origin remain plausible hypotheses for the first human SARS-CoV-2 infection; none reached certainty.
claims.quote: all agencies continue to assess that both a natural and laboratory-associated origin remain plausible hypotheses to explain the first human infection

claims.claim: The National Intelligence Council and four unnamed intelligence agencies assessed that natural exposure to an infected animal was the most likely scenario for the first human COVID-19 infection.
claims.quote: the National Intelligence Council and four unnamed agencies assessed that natural exposure to an infected animal was the most likely scenario for the first human infection

claims.claim: The Department of Energy and the FBI assessed that a laboratory-associated incident was more likely the cause of the pandemic's origin (splitting from the agencies favoring natural origin).
claims.quote: the Department of Energy and FBI's assessment was that a laboratory-associated incident was more likely the cause

claims.claim: The CIA and one unidentified agency remained unable to determine the precise origin, citing that both hypotheses rely on significant assumptions or face conflicting reporting.
claims.quote: remain unable to determine the precise origin of the COVID-19 pandemic, as both hypotheses rely on significant assumptions or face challenges with conflicting reporting

claims.claim: The declassified report was released in June 2023 as required by the COVID-19 Origin Act of 2023, which passed both chambers of Congress unanimously and was signed into law by President Biden.
claims.quote: The report's release was required by the Covid–19 Origin Act of 2023, which was passed unanimously by both chambers of Congress and signed into law by President Joe Biden.

title: Data are Insufficient to Confidently Root the SARS-CoV-2 Phylogenetic Tree (2025), Molecular Biology and Evolution

claims.claim: For SARS-CoV-2 the two standard rooting principles conflict: the earliest-collected sequences (mostly from people who visited or worked at the Huanan Market, i.e. lineage B) are NOT the sequences most similar to the bat-coronavirus outgroups, so outgroup-based rooting (which favors lineage A as ancestral) and date-based rooting (which favors the earliest, market-linked lineage B) point to different roots.
claims.quote: For SARS-CoV-2, these two basic rooting principles are in conflict: the first collected sequences (which are mostly from people who visited or worked at the Huanan Market) are not the sequences that are most similar to SARS-CoV-2's bat coronavirus relatives.

claims.claim: The root of the SARS-CoV-2 tree cannot be confidently placed between lineage A and lineage B with current genomic data, which means genomic/phylogenetic data alone cannot definitively establish whether the pandemic began with one or two independent introductions into humans.
claims.quote: the evidence is insufficient to confidently position the root of the evolutionary tree between lineage A and lineage B

claims.claim: The discrepancy between outgroup- and date-based rooting is attributable to demonstrable bias among the earliest available SARS-CoV-2 sequences, and this bias is why the root cannot be conclusively resolved with existing data.
claims.quote: Bloom demonstrated clear evidence of bias among the earliest available sequences, and explained why the root of the SARS-CoV-2 tree cannot be conclusively resolved with the current data.

claims.claim: The paper is a single-author peer-reviewed analysis by Jesse D. Bloom (Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center / HHMI) published in Molecular Biology and Evolution in 2025 (vol. 42, issue 6, article msaf118; DOI 10.1093/molbev/msaf118), accompanied by an interactive narrative letting readers examine the underlying sequence data.
claims.quote: "The Data are Insufficient to Confidently Root the SARS-CoV-2 Phylogenetic Tree" (DOI: 10.1093/molbev/msaf118) was published in 2025 by Jesse D Bloom from Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center.

title: Unwarranted Exclusion of Intermediate Lineage A-B SARS-CoV-2 Genomes Is Inconsistent with the Two-Spillover Hypothesis (2023), Microbiology Research

claims.claim: Lineages A and B are differentiated by two SNVs (at positions 8782 and 28144), so the two-spillover hypothesis of Pekar et al. logically requires that a single-SNV A-B intermediate genome existed in an unsampled animal host; finding such an intermediate in humans would falsify the two-spillover model.
claims.quote: The two lineages are differentiated by two SNVs; hence, a single-SNV A-B intermediate must have occurred in an unsampled animal host if the two-spillover hypothesis is correct. Confirmation of the existence of an intermediate A-B genome from humans would falsify their hypothesis of two spillovers.

claims.claim: Pekar et al. (2022) identified and excluded 20 A-B intermediate human genomes from their phylogenetic analysis supporting two separate zoonotic spillovers at the Huanan Seafood Market.
claims.quote: Pekar et al. identified and excluded 20 A-B intermediate genomes from their analysis... Pekar et al. proposed that SARS-CoV-2 was a zoonotic spillover that first infected humans in the Huanan Seafood Market in Wuhan, China, with two separate spillovers of the closely related lineages A and lineage B in a short period of time.

claims.claim: The exclusion criteria used by Pekar et al. — low read depth and assertion of erroneous base calls at lineage-defining positions 8782 and 28144 — are inconsistent with GISAID data showing most excluded genomes were sequenced to high average sequencing depth.
claims.quote: A variety of exclusion criteria were applied, including low read depth and the assertion of repeated erroneous base calls at lineage-defining positions 8782 and 28144. However, data from GISAID show that most of the genomes were sequenced to high average sequencing depth, appearing inconsistent with these criteria.

claims.claim: The authors conclude that the exclusion of the intermediate genomes is unfounded, which leaves the conclusion of two independent natural zoonoses unsupported.
claims.quote: The authors find that the exclusion of many of the intermediate genomes is unfounded, leaving the conclusion of two natural zoonoses unsupported.

title: U.S. Right to Know — American scientists misled Pentagon on research at the Wuhan Institute of Virology
Worobey et al. (2022), The Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market in Wuhan was the early epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic, Science

claims.claim: The earliest known COVID-19 cases from December 2019, including those with no reported link to the market, were geographically centered on the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market, statistically identifying it as the early epicenter of the pandemic.
claims.quote: The earliest human cases centered around the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market, and within the market, the data statistically located the earliest human cases to one section where vendors of live wild animals congregated and where virus-positive environmental samples concentrated.

claims.claim: SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples inside the market (73 positives, with three live-virus isolations) were spatially clustered in the section where vendors sold live wild/susceptible mammals, linking the virus to the wildlife trade rather than to seafood or human traffic generally.
claims.quote: 73 environmental samples tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and three live viruses were successfully isolated. SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.

claims.claim: The two early viral lineages (A and B) circulating before February 2020 reflect at least two separate cross-species (animal-to-human) introductions at the Huanan market, rather than a single spillover event.
claims.quote: Genomic diversity before February 2020 comprised two distinct viral lineages, A and B, which were the result of at least two separate cross-species transmission events into humans. Separate introductions of SARS-CoV-2 lineages A and B into humans occurred from likely infected animals at the Huanan market.

claims.claim: The authors conclude the pandemic's emergence occurred via the live wildlife trade in China, with the Huanan market as the origin point — supporting a natural zoonotic-spillover hypothesis over a lab-associated origin.
claims.quote: The analyses indicate that the emergence of SARS-CoV-2 occurred via the live wildlife trade in China, and show that the Huanan market was the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic.

claims.claim: Live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were being sold at the Huanan market in late 2019, establishing the physical presence of a plausible intermediate-host pathway at the identified epicenter.
claims.quote: Live SARS-CoV-2 susceptible mammals were sold at the market in late 2019, and within the market, SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.

title: Current Debate Regarding Laboratory and Natural Origin Hypotheses of SARS-CoV-2: A Critically Balanced Review (IntechOpen)
DOE and FBI Say Lab Origin of COVID Is 'Most Likely' — But Won't Say Why (Snopes)

claims.claim: As of the article's March 2023 publication, the U.S. Department of Energy assessed with 'low confidence' that a laboratory-associated incident was the 'most likely' origin of the first human SARS-CoV-2 infection, while the FBI assessed a lab origin with 'moderate confidence.'
claims.quote: The DOE assessed with 'low confidence' that a laboratory-associated incident was the 'most likely' source of the first human COVID-19 infection... One intelligence community element assessed with moderate confidence that the first human infection with SARS-CoV-2 most likely was the result of a laboratory-associated incident, probably involving experimentation, animal handling, or sampling by the Wuhan Institute of Virology.

claims.claim: The intelligence community was divided, with four IC elements plus the National Intelligence Council assessing (with low confidence) that natural zoonotic exposure was the most likely origin, contradicting the DOE and FBI lab-leak assessments.
claims.quote: Four intelligence community elements and the National Intelligence Council assessed with low confidence that the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection was most likely caused by natural exposure to an animal infected with it or a close progenitor virus.

claims.claim: The FBI and DOE lab-origin assessments are not based on scientific analysis of the virus but on circumstantial arguments, and the specific reasoning/evidence behind them was not publicly disclosed.
claims.quote: The assessments of both the FBI and DOE are not rooted in a scientific analysis of the virus or mechanisms linking SARS-CoV-2 to the WIV, but are circumstantial arguments about conditions being ideal for a potential laboratory release.

claims.claim: Based on the limited details released, the evidence supporting a laboratory-associated origin appeared to be largely or entirely circumstantial, and it is unclear what scientific analyses (if any) informed these intelligence assessments.
claims.quote: Based on the limited details released by the intelligence community, the evidence supporting a laboratory-associated incident appears to be largely if not entirely circumstantial.

title: A Critical Analysis of the Evidence for the SARS-CoV-2 Origin Hypotheses (mBio)

claims.claim: The authors conclude the two origin hypotheses are not equally probable and that the balance of evidence favors natural zoonotic origin, while acknowledging a definitive determination is unlikely with currently available data.
claims.quote: the two plausible possibilities for the origin of SARS-CoV-2 are not equally likely [...] a definitive determination of the origin is unlikely

claims.claim: Early SARS-CoV-2 isolates retained an intact furin cleavage site, whereas that site tends to be deleted during laboratory cell-culture handling, which the authors argue is evidence against introduction into humans after passage in laboratory cell culture.
claims.quote: the deletion of the furin cleavage site is expected when a virus undergoes laboratory handling, yet early isolates of SARS-CoV-2 show the furin cleavage site to be intact, arguing against introduction into humans after laboratory cell culture

claims.claim: Two genetically distinct early lineages (A and B), distinguishable by genome sequence, circulated in early Wuhan cases, which the authors interpret as consistent with multiple independent spillover events into humans.
claims.quote: Early cases in Wuhan involved two lineages, distinguishable by the viral genome sequence, suggesting that there were multiple events of spillover into humans

claims.claim: The intermediate host and the specific viral lineage that seeded the pandemic remain unidentified because the host niche harboring the direct precursors of SARS-CoV-2 has not been adequately sampled.
claims.quote: large gaps remain in our knowledge that preclude the identification of the viral lineage that seeded the pandemic or the intermediate host: the host niche harboring the direct precursors of SARS-CoV-2 has not yet been adequately sampled

claims.claim: Roughly one-quarter of the first 174 documented December 2019 cases were epidemiologically linked to the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market, supporting the market as an early focus of the outbreak.
claims.quote: Among the first 174 documented cases of SARS-CoV-2 infection in December 2019, approximately one-quarter had an association with the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market

title: Updated Assessment on COVID-19 Origins (ODNI, declassified)
Crits-Christoph, Levy, Pekar et al. (2024), Genetic tracing of market wildlife and viruses at the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic, Cell

claims.claim: Environmental samples from a specific wildlife stall in the southwest corner of the Huanan Seafood Market showed increased SARS-CoV-2 positivity, and wildlife DNA was identified in every SARS-CoV-2-positive sample from that stall.
claims.quote: We demonstrate that the SARS-CoV-2 genetic diversity linked to this market is consistent with market emergence and find increased SARS-CoV-2 positivity near and within a wildlife stall. We identify wildlife DNA in all SARS-CoV-2-positive samples from this stall.

claims.claim: The wildlife species whose DNA co-occurred with SARS-CoV-2-positive market samples include civets, bamboo rats, and raccoon dogs — species previously proposed as candidate intermediate hosts — with raccoon dogs and hoary bamboo rats being the most abundant mammalian wildlife detected.
claims.quote: including species such as civets, bamboo rats, and raccoon dogs, previously identified as possible intermediate hosts

claims.claim: Specific market objects associated with a wildlife stall (animal carts, a cage, and a hair/feather remover) tested SARS-CoV-2-positive and contained more mammalian wildlife DNA than human DNA, indicating the virus co-located with animals rather than merely with infected humans.
claims.quote: Animal carts, a cage, and a hair/feather remover from a wildlife stall tested positive for SARS-CoV-2, and there was more DNA from mammalian wildlife species in these samples than human DNA.

claims.claim: Both SARS-CoV-2 lineage A and lineage B were detected in market environmental samples, and the earliest lineage A cases were geographically close to the market — consistent with two spillover events at the market rather than a single introduction.
claims.quote: sequenced market-linked cases were of lineage B, but the earliest-known lineage A cases were close to the market, and both lineages A and B were detected in environmental samples at the market

claims.claim: The authors conclude that multiple independent lines of evidence corroborate a live wildlife market origin of the COVID-19 pandemic via the wildlife trade, rather than a lab-related origin.
claims.quote: the emergence of SARS-CoV-2 occurred via the live wildlife trade in China, and the Huanan market was the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic

title: Purported quantitative support for multiple introductions of SARS-CoV-2 into humans is an artefact of an imbalanced hypothesis-testing framework (2025)

claims.claim: Pekar et al.'s widely-cited genomic analysis claimed strong support (Bayes factor ~60) for two separate zoonotic introductions of SARS-CoV-2 into humans, underpinning the argument that the market outbreak resulted from multiple animal-to-human spillovers rather than one introduction.
claims.quote: A prominent report claimed substantial support for two introductions of SARS-CoV-2 into humans using a calculation that combined phylodynamic inferences and epidemic models... The published Bayes factor was 60, with which the authors claimed strong support for multiple introductions.

claims.claim: McCowan argues the claimed statistical support for two introductions is an artefact of an imbalanced hypothesis-testing framework in which the single-introduction model was held to more stringent acceptance conditions than the two-introduction model.
claims.quote: Inspection of the calculation identifies an imbalance in the hypothesis testing framework that confounds this result; the single-introduction model was tested against more stringent conditions than the two-introduction model.

claims.claim: When the two-introduction model is subjected to the same conditions imposed on the single-introduction model, the quantitative support for multiple introductions disappears, undermining a load-bearing genomic argument for zoonotic origin.
claims.quote: The author shows that when the two-introduction model is tested against the same conditions, the support disappears.

claims.claim: The specific asymmetries driving the inflated Bayes factor are identifiable as a relative-clade-size condition, an evolutionary-separation condition, and a root-shape condition applied unequally to the competing models.
claims.quote: The paper identifies imbalanced conditions including a relative-clade size condition, an evolutionary separation condition, and a root shape condition, and evaluates their effects on the Bayes factor.

title: Liu, Liu et al. (2023), Surveillance of SARS-CoV-2 at the Huanan Seafood Market, Nature

claims.claim: SARS-CoV-2 was detected by RT-qPCR in 73 environmental samples from the Huanan market, but in none of the 457 animal samples collected (from 18 species), meaning no infected animal was directly identified.
claims.quote: Using RT-qPCR, SARS-CoV-2 was detected in 73 environmental samples, but none of the animal samples.

claims.claim: Some SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples also contained genetic material from animals susceptible to the virus, including raccoon dogs, establishing co-location of virus and susceptible-species DNA at the market.
claims.quote: some environmental samples contained genetic material from animals susceptible to SARS-CoV-2, including raccoon dogs

claims.claim: Both viral lineages were represented in the market environment: lineage A (defined by the 8782T and 28144C markers) was recovered from an environmental sample, in addition to lineage B.
claims.quote: SARS-CoV-2 lineage A (8782T and 28144C) was found in an environmental sample.

claims.claim: The original authors interpreted their data cautiously, concluding that presence of susceptible-animal DNA in positive samples does not prove those animals were infected, and that human-to-animal reverse transmission could not be excluded because sampling occurred in January 2020, after human cases had been reported and the market was closed.
claims.quote: even if animals were infected, it cannot be ruled out that human-to-animal reverse transmission occurred after human beings were infected with the virus, since the sampling period was in January, 2020, after the time when human cases were reported

claims.claim: Sampling occurred only after the market's closure: 923 environmental samples were collected from 1 January 2020 and 457 animal samples from 18 January 2020, so the dataset cannot capture the live-animal state of the market before closure.
claims.quote: From 1 January 2020, after closure of the market, 923 samples were collected from the environment. From 18 January, 457 samples were collected from 18 species of animal, comprising unsold contents of refrigerators and freezers, swabs from stray animals and the contents of a fish tank.

title: The Intercept — NIH Documents Provide New Evidence U.S. Funded Gain-of-Function Research in Wuhan
